quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Free energy (ΔG)"?

options.text: A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification
The energy available to do work; ΔG  0 (endergonic, requires energy input), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium)
No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution
One gene affects the expression of another gene at a different locus (e.g., coat color in Labrador retrievers: B/b for color, E/e for deposition)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Free energy (ΔG)" is defined as: The energy available to do work; ΔG  0 (endergonic, requires energy input), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "ATP (adenosine triphosphate)"?

options.text: No mutation, no migration, random mating, no selection, large population size; violations drive evolution
The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)
Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers (aneuploidy)
The cell's primary energy currency; hydrolysis of the terminal phosphate releases -30.5 kJ/mol used to power endergonic reactions

quiz_questions.explanation: "ATP (adenosine triphosphate)" is defined as: The cell's primary energy currency; hydrolysis of the terminal phosphate releases -30.5 kJ/mol used to power endergonic reactions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy coupling"?

options.text: Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate
5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes
Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers (aneuploidy)
ATP production driven by the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane; H⁺ flows through ATP synthase from lumen to stroma

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy coupling" is defined as: Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Entropy"?

options.text: Double-membrane organelle where cellular respiration produces ATP; has own circular DNA, supporting the endosymbiotic theory
A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes
A signaling molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor) that binds specifically to a receptor protein
The variety of life at three levels: genetic diversity (within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance), and ecosystem diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Entropy" is defined as: A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Metabolism"?

options.text: A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
The totality of an organism's chemical reactions; catabolism (breakdown, releases energy) and anabolism (synthesis, requires energy)
A species or group outside the clade being studied, used to root the phylogenetic tree and determine which characters are ancestral vs. derived
The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix

quiz_questions.explanation: "Metabolism" is defined as: The totality of an organism's chemical reactions; catabolism (breakdown, releases energy) and anabolism (synthesis, requires energy). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy available to do work; ΔG  0 (endergonic, requires energy input), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium)"?

options.text: Frameshift mutation
Cohesion
Free energy (ΔG)
Genetic drift

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Free energy (ΔG)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The cell's primary energy currency; hydrolysis of the terminal phosphate releases -30.5 kJ/mol used to power endergonic reactions"?

options.text: ATP (adenosine triphosphate)
Ecosystem services
Hardy-Weinberg conditions
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "ATP (adenosine triphosphate)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate"?

options.text: Adaptive radiation
Latitudinal diversity gradient
Energy coupling
Independent assortment

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy coupling".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes"?

options.text: Missense mutation
Mitosis
Steroid
Entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Entropy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The totality of an organism's chemical reactions; catabolism (breakdown, releases energy) and anabolism (synthesis, requires energy)"?

options.text: CRISPR-Cas9
Metabolism
Habitat destruction
Net primary productivity (NPP)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Metabolism".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Energy and Metabolism is accurate?

options.text: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid (2n) daughter cells for growth and repair, while meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid (n) gametes for sexual reproduction.
Energy and Metabolism describes how cells manage energy through coupled reactions and metabolic pathways.

The three stages—reception (ligand binding), transduction (signal relay/amplification through phosphorylation cascades), and response (gene expression, enzyme activation, or cell movement)—allow cells to respond to their environment.
The three modes of natural selection are directional (shifts the mean), stabilizing (reduces variation around the mean), and disruptive (favors extremes).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Metabolism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: The laws of thermodynamics govern biological systems: energy is conserved (1st law) and entropy increases in spontaneous processes (2nd law).
Recombination frequency between linked genes (calculated from testcross offspring) indicates the distance between genes on a chromosome, measured in centimorgans (cM) or map units.
Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Three turns of the Calvin cycle fix 3 CO₂ to produce one net G3P molecule, consuming 9 ATP and 6 NADPH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Metabolism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.
Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid (2n) daughter cells for growth and repair, while meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid (n) gametes for sexual reproduction.
Cell Division (Mitosis and Meiosis) are the two types of nuclear division.
Free energy change (ΔG) determines reaction spontaneity: exergonic reactions (ΔG  0) require energy input.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Metabolism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: Minimum viable population (MVP), effective population size (Ne), and genetic rescue inform conservation management.
ATP hydrolysis (ΔG = -30.5 kJ/mol) couples to endergonic reactions to drive cellular work.
DNA Replication and Transcription describes how genetic information stored in DNA is copied (replication) and read into mRNA (transcription).
Genomic technologies (DNA sequencing, gene editing with CRISPR-Cas9, and bioinformatics) have revolutionized biology, medicine, agriculture, and forensics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Metabolism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.
Oxygen is the final electron acceptor, forming water.
Energy and Metabolism describes how cells manage energy through coupled reactions and metabolic pathways.
Enzyme specificity arises from the precise 3D shape of the active site, which is determined by the amino acid sequence.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Metabolism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: Telomere shortening with each cell division limiting cell lifespan; telomerase activation in cancer cells enables unlimited replication (immortality)
Antibiotic targeting of bacterial ribosomes: tetracycline blocks the A-site, chloramphenicol inhibits peptidyl transferase—both exploit differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic ribosomes
Firefly bioluminescence using ATP to power the oxidation of luciferin, converting chemical energy directly to light with remarkable efficiency
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: Metabolic syndrome and diabetes: dysregulation of glucose metabolism affecting cellular respiration, insulin signaling, and energy storage pathways
Lead poisoning: lead (Pb²⁺) replaces zinc in enzymes, acting as a noncompetitive inhibitor and disrupting nervous system, kidney, and blood cell function
Muscle contraction powered by ATP hydrolysis changing the conformation of myosin heads along actin filaments (cross-bridge cycling)
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an extra copy of chromosome 21 in all cells

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Energy and Metabolism applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Active transport of ions (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) using ATP to pump 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per cycle, maintaining the resting membrane potential essential for nerve impulses
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years
Feedback inhibition in the cholesterol synthesis pathway: statins mimic the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, acting as competitive inhibitors to lower cholesterol levels

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Energy and Metabolism?

options.text: Firefly bioluminescence using ATP to power the oxidation of luciferin, converting chemical energy directly to light with remarkable efficiency
Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands illustrating adaptive radiation: 14 species with different beak shapes evolved from a single ancestor to exploit different food sources
Ice insulation of lakes in winter: because ice (density 0.917 g/cm³) floats, aquatic organisms survive below the frozen surface
Prion diseases (mad cow, CJD) where misfolded proteins induce normal proteins to refold incorrectly, demonstrating the critical importance of protein conformation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Energy and Metabolism in practice?

options.text: Mitochondrial diseases caused by mutations in ETC complex genes, affecting energy-demanding tissues (brain, heart, muscles) and causing conditions like Leigh syndrome
Muscle contraction powered by ATP hydrolysis changing the conformation of myosin heads along actin filaments (cross-bridge cycling)

Wolf reintroduction into Yellowstone (1995) demonstrating trophic cascades: wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing riparian vegetation recovery, which stabilized stream banks and increased beaver populations
Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Metabolism.
